svg.text: 08 - New Zealand Pilot Domain Intelligence
What this covers: destination-specific rules Buddi must internalize for high-quality planning and adaptation.
Why NZ Is the Right Pilot
High condition sensitivity + dense attraction diversity + long road-trip patterns make adaptation value obvious and measurable.
Climate + Geography Truths
- Maritime climate with rapid shifts ("four seasons in one day").
- Strong west-to-east weather front movement.
- Southern Alps rain shadow creates extreme regional contrasts.
- UV is unusually intense in summer; protection guidance is mandatory.
- Southern hemisphere seasons are reversed for many travelers.
- North vs South Island climate behavior differs materially for itinerary design.
Operational weather-window strategy
Track fronts, identify post-front clear windows, and relocate weather-critical activities accordingly.
Use confidence-weighted language; avoid over-certainty on long horizons.
Safety-critical contexts
Tongariro and exposed alpine tracks: hard weather gates, not soft suggestions.
Road closure/chain requirements in winter routes must feed day-level logistics.
Activity-Specific Condition Intelligence
Milford Sound
Rain can improve experience (waterfalls/mood); avoid simplistic "rain = bad" logic.
Tongariro Crossing
Wind/visibility/wind-chill and volcanic alerts determine go/no-go safety threshold.
Glacier heli-hikes
Cloud ceiling is dominant risk; schedule with backup-day logic.
Stargazing and aurora
Cloud cover and moon phase are binary quality drivers; Kp index influences viability.
Whale watching and coastal tours
Sea state can flip from feasible to canceled quickly; maintain same-area alternatives.
Kayaking
Wind usually matters more than rain; align guidance with local operator constraints.
Tide + Transport + Local Ops
Tide-dependent examples
Cathedral Cove / Hot Water Beach / coastal walks rely on low-tide windows.
Schedule tide-critical activities first, then fill non-critical slots around them.
Storm surge caveat
Pressure drops and onshore wind can invalidate deterministic tide assumptions.
Transport complexity in NZ
Campervan/rental car dominant; ferries, domestic flights, and weather disruptions affect plan validity.
Route design must include realistic drive-time rules and remote-fuel constraints.
Data sources to trust most
MetService, DOC, NIWA, NZTA/Waka Kotahi, GeoNet as safety/authority backbone.
Open APIs enrich but should not override official safety advisories.
Pilot Scope and Expansion Logic
MVP scope: both islands, strongest 50-60 attractions, summer/shoulder seasons, key condition monitoring stack.
Out of scope initially: full winter specialization, full booking integrations, multi-language/social layers.
Pilot success criteria
planning quality ratings, swap acceptance rates, guidance usefulness, condition accuracy, coverage depth, companion engagement.
Globalization leverage
If system handles NZ volatility, same architecture can extend to Iceland/Patagonia/Norway/Scotland/Japan with model/data adaptation.
Reusable primitives: condition profiles, swap engine, transport templates, cultural-protocol metadata, remote-area logic.
Implementation mistakes to avoid in pilot
- generic weather advice without regional nuance
- no backup options for high-cancel-risk activities
- tide-insensitive coastal scheduling
- over-aggressive adaptation that ignores user disruption cost
Next recommended read: 09 - Roadmap and Implementation Order.
